steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student's hypothesis: New Caledonian nautilids lay eggs at 120 m; Fijian nautilids lay eggs at 170 m; since temperature decreases with depth, New Caledonian eggs are in warmer water. To weaken this, we need a finding that breaks the link between depth and temperature — something that could make 170 m in Fiji as warm or warmer than 120 m in New Caledonia.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — New Caledonia nautilids tend to migrate upward to warmer water immediately after hatching, whereas Fijian nautilids do not.: concerns post-hatching behavior, not egg-laying temperature. Irrelevant to the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — The water temperature at a given depth tends to be higher in waters near Fiji than it is at that depth in waters near New Caledonia.: if Fiji waters are systematically warmer at every depth, then 170 m in Fiji could be just as warm as — or warmer than — 120 m in New Caledonia. The depth-to-temperature assumption collapses. This directly weakens the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice C — There aren't enough suitable hatching areas at a depth of at least 170 meters in New Caledonia for nautilids to lay their eggs at that depth.: gives an alternative reason for the shallower depth but doesn't undermine the temperature conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — The water temperature at a given depth tends to be lower in waters near Fiji than it is at that depth in waters near New Caledonia.: this would make Fijian water even colder at 170 m, actually strengthening the hypothesis. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
